author: @AmeenAbedrabbo

title: FAU Civic literacy test Preparation # 1
body:
Hello! Just finished the FCLE at FSCJ! Here’s some tips that will help you tremendously.

1- the test will give you A LOT of questions related to the amendments, and Articles 1-6!!! I didn’t know about the articles but I still managed because I knew the amendments!

2- they won’t ask you any easy questions!! Think of the hardest ones you can imagine and they’ll throw it at you.

3- they’ll cover amendment uses, not the actual amendment. They take a line from an amendment or a president speech and ask you what amendments it follows.

4- focus on court cases by learning it the simplest way possible, each case won’t be on there but there’s gonna be one from each amendment.

5- really really you won’t get what you expect on the test, but just learn the basics and you won’t have a single problem, just really pay attention to what they mention in their speeches / or articles.

6- lastly pay attention to names!!! Names are important too!

title: FAU Civic literacy test Preparation # 1
body:
Just passed thanks to you and these videos! Tips for those about to take the exam…
1) Watch ALL THREE videos. This is the only thing I studied and scored high.
2) Over 50% of the exam were the cases and amendments, and how they relate to each other. Commit those to memory if nothing else.
3) Slow down and read the questions carefully. I almost got tripped up a couple times due to the wording of the exam.
4) About 5/80 questions on my exam were about specific Articles and random quotes that correspond to them, if you feel the need to study for those.
Thank you for making these videos to help us! 🤍

author: @Von-b7v

title: FAU Civic literacy test Preparation # 1
body:
I just finished the FCLE about 10 minutes ago (Feb 2024) and I’m driving home writing my brain dump. This exam prep is perfectly tailored to the content of the FCLE. It makes sense too, since the presenter of this video says she took the test multiple times to learn what content to teach. Of the 80 questions, about 30 were case law, and I didn’t see a single “case” that she didn’t talk about in video two. Every single case she teaches is relevant to the test.

The “memory cheats” she mentions are also very valuable. MAPP v OHIO = 4 letters = fourth amendment. I really remembered it that way for the test.

The remaining 50 questions were mostly asking any of the following:

Verbiage or identifying from a historical document
Questions about federalism, Supreme Court, and what rulings effected what amendments. It was amendment heavy.

Amendments 1, 3, 4, 5, 8, 14, 15 were mentioned on my test.

So here’s my tip:

Get some paper and turn this video on speed 1.5x and just pause it to take notes. Anything she emphasizes, be sure to write.

Know the precession of the historical documents, this will help you deduce an answer. Mayflower compact, articles of confederation, common sense, etc etc. know the order.

Dates also helped me figure out answers. I knew court cases from 1960’s didn’t have anything to do with James Madison (who lived in the late 1700s).

Overall I can confirm that this study guide video is both extremely relevant to the test but also does NOT include any other unrelated info. This instructor sticks very efficiently to what you need to know.

Lastly, this test is pretty easy. The questions aren’t tricks and usually 1 of the 4 multiple choice answers is the “obviously wrong” choice.

10/10 thank you to this instructor.

author: @nataliaa1105

title: FAU Civic Literacy Test Prep # 2
body:
These FAU workshop videos for the FCLE were very useful! I personally attended to my school's workshops for the FCLE, but they only told me to take the official practice test and provided scattered resources ranging from links to websites, books, and a CrashCourse playlist. This YouTube playlist really pinpoints the main topics and details that would be covered in the FCLE that wasn't covered by my history course. In my history course, we only focused on some of the landmark supreme court cases and some laws passed in the 1900s, which doesn't go over the foundation of U.S. American history very much (although my professor did give us 2 Quizlet decks to review for the FCLE for any additional information not covered in her course to help prepare for the exam). These workshop videos was very informative about these basics and follows the standard guidelines for the FCLE.

After taking the exam, I mainly encountered some questions about the Plessy v. Ferguson (1896), Brown v. Board of Education (1954), Engel v. Vitale (1962), Wisconsin v. Yoder (1972), and Korematsu v. U.S. (1944) landmark supreme court cases. In regards to amendments, it mainly focused on the first and fourth ones (it is best to be familiar to all 27 amendments). I also noticed that there was a question on the 3/5 Compromise as well. My only complaint is that the all of the videos didn't really cover everything that was going to be on the exam, including the purpose of the articles of the Constitution and the role of the vice president, which were in the exam.

For example, in the FCLE that I took, it asked what article in the Constitution that involves instructions for the amending process (in this case, it is Article V). Because of this, I had to go refer to the Supplemental Guide and YouTube to take other extra notes for the FCLE. I highly recommend watching a full study guide review video from a YouTuber named "govdogs" — he covers most of the aspects that would be covered in the FCLE based on the official supplemental guide of the FCLE (such as the articles, policies & laws, and other landmark supreme court cases not covered by these videos). Other than that, I took notes on both FAU videos and did all the practice questions at the end of the videos (and the practice exam at the beginning for video #3). After that, I managed to pass the FCLE! This meant that I scored a 60% or higher on the exam. From this, the workshop videos were really helpful! Thanks!

author: @gfeast4518

title: Florida State University - 10 Things I Wish I Knew Before Attending
body: Omg he isn't lying about Alumni Village not being in the best area. When I lived there when I was a gradate student, there was a crack house literally across the street from the bus stop outside of the village's fence. But I actually never had a problem. Tbh you kinda had to take a nole or city bus to get to school anyway, unless you were okay with walking about 45min to get to your classes. Sadly, the Village in 2014 closed due to the cost of projected renovations. I was heartbroken when the final decision was made because I had fond memories of living there. Quiet and quaint and full of history (they were built after the war to help veterans go back to school and have a place to live with their families). It wasn't fancy by ANY means, but it was sturdy and had the basic necessities...Im a low maintenance gal, so I never had a reason to complain. Plus there was always security there because there were two daycares on campus. Never had an issue with anything popping off around there. It's strange now to drive by and see nothing but a wisp of what used to be....alas. I will be going back for another graduate degree in the fall...I bleed Garnet and Gold. 😎
author: @poshpearls23

title: Florida State University - 10 Things I Wish I Knew Before Attending
body: Don't sleep on my hometown​.Even though it can be boring at times, but when it's a party going on or concert in town your going to have a good time. I've had friends from Miami that always loved to come to Tally​ for the weekend to party, they loved it. Also I live in Central FLA now, and Everytime I tell someone​ that I'm from Tally they. Treat me like a star, they always talk about the good times they had in Tally back in their days..lol  Tally is what you make it.

title: A Day In The Life: FAU College Edition
body: I never had the opportunity to go to college.  I came from a poor family and wasn't given the best direction through HS, so I chose to serve my country instead.  I have never regretted my decision as it was all I had.  It has worked out for me as I have been very successful after the service in my career and have been blessed with a beautiful family.  That said, I've always wondered what it would be like to attend college even for a day.  Your video gave me an idea, so thank you for sharing.
author: @daniels4227
